Name: Development of Experimental Low-Cost Rain Gauges and their Evaluation During a High Intensity Storm Event
Abstract: The measurement of rainfall via ground sensors is fundamental in a variety of hydrological applications, including rainfall-runoff simulations, basin water balance and flood forecasting. The tipping bucket rain gauge (TBR) constitutes the most common type of automatic gauge for the measurement of rainfall intensity. The objective of this work is the development of low-cost and reliable rain gauges, including their data logger, which could be installed at remote, rural areas, in order to supplement with rainfall data the limited or non-existing network of hydrological stations. To achieve this target, two experimental TBRs with diameters of 20 cm (RG20) and 28 cm (RG28) were developed. Electronic boards Arduino UNO and Raspberry Pi were used for their data logger. The measurements of RG20 and RG28 were compared with those of a high quality rain gauge ARG100 and a daily non-recording rain gauge. The cyclone Daniel on 06-09-2023 caused an intermittent storm event in the city of Athens, Greece, which was measured by all three TBRs for purposes of evaluation. The results showed that the variations between ARG100 and RG28 were lower than 6%, while the variations between ARG100 and RG20 were about 10% during a few time intervals of high rainfall intensity. The return period of the storm event were estimated at 43, 59 and 45 years for rainfall durations of 10, 20 and 30 min, respectively.

Name: THE BLIND SIDE OF A RAIN GAUGES NETWORK: INTRODUCTORY THEORETICAL APPROACH, WITH A FIRST EXAMPLE OF APPLICATION.
On acoustic monitoring of rainfall intensity
Abstract: Monti & Ntalampiras (EUSIPCO 2025) optimize an audio pipeline on the SARID dataset: low-pass filtering, FFT window sweep, meteorological auxiliary features, and a stacking ensemble of transformer regressors on MFCC/Mel/STFT features.
What_They_Do: Monti & Ntalampiras (EUSIPCO 2025) optimize an audio pipeline on the SARID dataset: low-pass filtering, FFT window sweep, meteorological auxiliary features, and a stacking ensemble of transformer regressors on MFCC/Mel/STFT features.
Results: Stacking ensemble R2=0.787, MAE=0.52 mm/h, RMSE=0.837 mm/h - beats the SARID baseline (0.765/0.563/0.88). Rain information concentrated below ~2000 Hz; 4096-sample FFT window best for MFCC; naive concatenation of met features did NOT help.
Inference: Published SOTA on SARID is ~0.79 R2, achieved with the same transformer+stacking strategy this project uses; even optimized pipelines saturate near 0.79 on curated single-site data, and meteorological feature fusion needs a smarter strategy than concatenation.
Supports_This_Project: Validates our stacking-ensemble design, gives the real SOTA number to benchmark against, suggests <2 kHz band-limiting and 4096 FFT window as concrete experiments, and its future-work section (no-rain samples, imbalance, hierarchical detect-then-estimate) is exactly what this project already does.

Name: Estimating rainfall intensity based on surveillance audio and deep-learning (SARID)
Abstract: Wang et al. (2024) build SARID, the first public dataset for continuous rainfall-intensity regression from surveillance audio (12,066 four-second clips from 6 events, annotated with intensity and environment), and train an MFCC+Transformer baseline.
What_They_Do: Wang et al. (2024) build SARID, the first public dataset for continuous rainfall-intensity regression from surveillance audio (12,066 four-second clips from 6 events, annotated with intensity and environment), and train an MFCC+Transformer baseline.
Results: Baseline MAE ~0.88 mm/h and correlation 0.765 against ground truth; demonstrates audio as an all-weather, low-data-volume complement to video rainfall estimation.
Inference: Audio alone supports continuous intensity regression, but their score comes from curated, single-site, rain-only data - an upper bound relative to realistic deployments.
Supports_This_Project: THE benchmark and architectural template for our DL track; our novelty (19 messy field campaigns, 85/15 dry/rain imbalance, joint detection+regression) is defined as the gap this paper leaves open.

Name: Towards High Resolution Weather Monitoring with Sound Data
Abstract: Coban, Perra & Mandel (2023) classify rain/wind/temperature from ecoacoustic recordings using coarse MERRA-2 satellite reanalysis as weak labels, avoiding manual annotation.
What_They_Do: Coban, Perra & Mandel (2023) classify rain/wind/temperature from ecoacoustic recordings using coarse MERRA-2 satellite reanalysis as weak labels, avoiding manual annotation.
Results: Rain is the best-predicted variable; classifiers trained from noisy MERRA-2 labels are more accurate than raw MERRA-2 itself and approach fully supervised performance with almost no manual labels.
Inference: Weak supervision from coarse global weather products can replace co-located gauges for training; models can out-perform their own noisy teachers.
Supports_This_Project: Offers a scaling path: future recordings without a co-located tipping bucket can be pseudo-labeled from reanalysis/nearby stations to grow the training corpus; confirms rain is the most acoustically learnable weather variable.

Name: An Innovative Acoustic Rain Gauge Based on Convolutional Neural Networks
Abstract: Avanzato & Beritelli (2020) apply a CNN directly to raw audio (3 s windows, 100 ms hop) to classify rain into 7 intensity classes from 'No rain' to 'Cloudburst' on low-cost hardware.
What_They_Do: Avanzato & Beritelli (2020) apply a CNN directly to raw audio (3 s windows, 100 ms hop) to classify rain into 7 intensity classes from 'No rain' to 'Cloudburst' on low-cost hardware.
Results: 75% average accuracy over 7 classes, rising to 93% when adjacent-class confusions are tolerated; enables fast heavy-rain alerting on cheap devices.
Inference: Pre-SARID audio work capped out at classification; confusions are mostly between adjacent classes, implying a smooth audio-to-intensity mapping favorable to regression.
Supports_This_Project: Documents the classification-only prior art our research-gap claim cites; its 'No rain' class is precedent for our detection stage; raw-waveform CNN is an ablation candidate against MFCC input.

Name: Development of Improved Acoustic Disdrometer Through Utilization of Machine Learning Algorithm
Abstract: Cruz et al. (TENCON 2018) build an acoustic disdrometer from 4 piezo sensors + Arduino + ZigBee and add a KNN classifier so it can tell rainfall-intensity categories apart from ambient noise.
What_They_Do: Cruz et al. (TENCON 2018) build an acoustic disdrometer from 4 piezo sensors + Arduino + ZigBee and add a KNN classifier so it can tell rainfall-intensity categories apart from ambient noise.
Results: KNN model reaches 89.95% accuracy in categorizing rainfall intensity vs ambient noise.
Inference: Even simple ML on piezo-acoustic signals solves the rain-vs-noise discrimination that made older acoustic disdrometers unusable.
Supports_This_Project: Direct precedent for our is-it-raining classifier and for the claim that ML fixes ambient-noise confusion; cheap piezo hardware reference for deployment variants.

Name: Design and development of a novel acoustic rain sensor with automated telemetry
Abstract: Guico et al. (2018) build a solar-powered, weather-proof standalone acoustic rain sensor (mic under a metal dome, acoustic power as rain proxy) with SMS/mobile telemetry, field-deployed next to standard gauges in the Philippines.
What_They_Do: Guico et al. (2018) build a solar-powered, weather-proof standalone acoustic rain sensor (mic under a metal dome, acoustic power as rain proxy) with SMS/mobile telemetry, field-deployed next to standard gauges in the Philippines.
Results: Field deployment shows acoustic signal power tracks rain events seen by co-located reference instruments; demonstrates a practical telemetered low-cost sensor for sub-km rainfall monitoring.
Inference: The engineering wrapper (power, waterproofing, telemetry) is solved and cheap; the bottleneck is the intensity model - the part this project supplies.
Supports_This_Project: Deployment blueprint for turning our model into a field instrument and evidence for the low-cost dense-network motivation.

Name: A New Device for Continuous, Real-Time Acoustic Measurement of Rain Inclination
Abstract: Dunkerley (2025) introduces an inexpensive acoustic device measuring wind-driven-rain inclination angle in real time, plus rainfall duration/intermittency at high resolution; demonstrated in tropical trade-wind showers.
What_They_Do: Dunkerley (2025) introduces an inexpensive acoustic device measuring wind-driven-rain inclination angle in real time, plus rainfall duration/intermittency at high resolution; demonstrated in tropical trade-wind showers.
Results: Continuous real-time resolution of rain inclination during showers with no extra apparatus; methods paper without accuracy benchmarking yet.
Inference: Wind-driven rain hits obliquely at super-terminal speeds, so impact sound depends on wind, not just rain rate - and that wind effect is itself acoustically observable.
Supports_This_Project: Explains physically why wind is our identified next lever (it changes the acoustic signature), and hints wind could be estimated from the same audio instead of needing an anemometer.

Name: Acoustical Rainfall Analysis: Rainfall Drop Size Distribution Using the Underwater Sound Field
Abstract: Nystuen (1996) formally inverts the underwater ambient sound field to retrieve the raindrop size distribution, using lab-derived per-drop-size sound signatures, validated at the AOML rain gauge facility.
File: research papers\atot-1520-0426_1996_013_0074_arards_2_0_co_2.pdf (scan; text copy also in library)
What_They_Do: Nystuen (1996) formally inverts the underwater ambient sound field to retrieve the raindrop size distribution, using lab-derived per-drop-size sound signatures, validated at the AOML rain gauge facility.
Results: Over dozens of events across six months, the ARA algorithm gives excellent estimates of rain rate, accumulation and radar reflectivity, resolving DSD variations at 5-10 s resolution.
Inference: Rain sound is a superposition of drop-size-specific signatures: audio carries full rain microphysics, so intensity regression from sound has a causal physical basis.
Supports_This_Project: The physical foundation of the whole project idea; motivates spectral features aligned with drop-size frequency bands and a long-term DSD-from-audio stretch goal.

Name: Relative Performance of Automatic Rain Gauges under Different Rainfall Conditions
Abstract: Nystuen (1999) compares six automatic gauge types (tipping bucket, weighing, capacitance, optical, disdrometer, acoustic) side by side for 17 months (~800 events) in Miami across rainfall regimes.
What_They_Do: Nystuen (1999) compares six automatic gauge types (tipping bucket, weighing, capacitance, optical, disdrometer, acoustic) side by side for 17 months (~800 events) in Miami across rainfall regimes.
Results: All gauges intercorrelate >=0.9 at 1-min rates, biases <10%, but each fails somewhere: TB & disdrometer bias low >100 mm/h, optical biases high, capacitance/TB noisy <2 mm/h, acoustic masked by >3.5 mm drops, acoustic & disdrometer bias low above 5 m/s wind.
Inference: Ground truth is not absolute: our tipping-bucket labels carry known condition-dependent biases, so part of model residual error is label error.
Supports_This_Project: Quantifies label noise in our ground truth, identifies the regimes (extreme rain, drizzle, wind) where acoustic sensing needs targeted data/features, and backs the wind-undercatch claims in the handbook.

Name: Passive Acoustic Detection and Measurement of Rainfall at Sea
Abstract: Ma & Nystuen (2005) turn TAO-array hydrophones (90+ buoy-months) into calibrated acoustic rain gauges: absolute self-calibration using the universal wind-sound spectrum, acoustic discrimination of rain vs wind vs noise, then a single-frequency rainfall-rate algorithm.
What_They_Do: Ma & Nystuen (2005) turn TAO-array hydrophones (90+ buoy-months) into calibrated acoustic rain gauges: absolute self-calibration using the universal wind-sound spectrum, acoustic discrimination of rain vs wind vs noise, then a single-frequency rainfall-rate algorithm.
Results: Acoustic rainfall accumulations match collocated R.M. Young gauges and TRMM 3B42 from hourly to yearly scales, and the method transfers to two other ocean sites.
Inference: A classify-first then-regress cascade is the proven acoustic architecture, and self-calibration against a universal ambient reference solves cross-sensor variation.
Supports_This_Project: Precedent for our detection-to-regression pipeline; wind-based self-calibration maps onto our cross-campaign normalization problem (we already removed harmful per-clip peak normalization).

Name: Raindrop Size Distribution and Sampling Size Errors
Abstract: Joss & Waldvogel (1969) derive theoretically (exponential DSD + Poisson drop counts) the standard deviation of rain rate R and reflectivity Z estimated from finite drop samples.
What_They_Do: Joss & Waldvogel (1969) derive theoretically (exponential DSD + Poisson drop counts) the standard deviation of rain rate R and reflectivity Z estimated from finite drop samples.
Results: Small samples give badly noisy R and Z: at 1 mm/h, ~1 m2 of surface for 1 s is needed for Z within 20% of the mean at 68% probability; sampling error alone explains much Z-R variability.
Inference: Any short-window rain estimate (10 s audio clip vs 0.2 mm-per-tip bucket) has an irreducible variance floor from counting finitely many drops.
Supports_This_Project: Explains a structural part of our residual error (quantized, sampling-noisy light-rain labels), justifies temporal aggregation, and defends realistic-data R2 against curated benchmarks.

Name: Systematic Study of Weather Variables for Rainfall Detection
Abstract: Manandhar et al. (2018) run PCA on 7 weather/time variables (RH, solar, temperature, dew point, GPS-PWV, day-of-year, time-of-day) from a Singapore station and use leading PCs to separate rain vs no-rain.
What_They_Do: Manandhar et al. (2018) run PCA on 7 weather/time variables (RH, solar, temperature, dew point, GPS-PWV, day-of-year, time-of-day) from a Singapore station and use leading PCs to separate rain vs no-rain.
Results: Four PCs explain 85% of variance; the first two already separate rain/no-rain; all 7 variables contribute comparably to detection.
Inference: Cheap scalar weather variables carry real rain/no-rain signal but are largely redundant; a few components suffice.
Supports_This_Project: Tells us which auxiliary weather features would add detection skill (and that they should be decorrelated) - useful for suppressing false positives in dry noisy clips.

Name: Analyzing the Impact of Meteorological Parameters on Rainfall Prediction
Abstract: Pathan et al. (2021) correlate 5 years of NOAA station data (Michigan) and use ML feature selection to rank meteorological features for rainfall prediction.
What_They_Do: Pathan et al. (2021) correlate 5 years of NOAA station data (Michigan) and use ML feature selection to rank meteorological features for rainfall prediction.
Results: Wind-related features correlate positively with precipitation (r~0.24-0.26) and rank among the most important predictors.
Inference: Wind is not only a gauge/audio confounder - it is itself informative about precipitation occurrence.
Supports_This_Project: Reinforces wind speed as the right next feature: it both confounds the acoustic signal and independently predicts rain.

Name: Measurement of Underwater Acoustic Energy Radiated by Single Raindrops
Abstract: Liu et al. (2021) derive a dipole-radiation formula for per-raindrop underwater sound energy and measure it in a reverberation tank across drop sizes, modeling kinetic-to-acoustic conversion efficiency.
What_They_Do: Liu et al. (2021) derive a dipole-radiation formula for per-raindrop underwater sound energy and measure it in a reverberation tank across drop sizes, modeling kinetic-to-acoustic conversion efficiency.
Results: A predictive model of average radiated sound energy for any drop diameter; total rainfall sound energy predictable from the drop size distribution.
Inference: Acoustic energy scales with drop size/kinetic energy quantifiably: 'louder = heavier rain' is monotonic but DSD-dependent, not a simple function of rain rate.
Supports_This_Project: Grounds intensity regression in drop physics and explains why energy features are strong but imperfect - motivating spectral-shape features alongside band energies.

Name: Measurement of Three-Dimensional Unsteady Flows Using an Inexpensive Multiple Disk Probe
Abstract: Green et al. (2001) develop a cheap velocimeter of three orthogonal pressure-transducer disks measuring all 3 wind velocity components in highly 3-D flows.
What_They_Do: Green et al. (2001) develop a cheap velocimeter of three orthogonal pressure-transducer disks measuring all 3 wind velocity components in highly 3-D flows.
Results: Flat response to 3-4 Hz and velocity accuracy better than +/-0.3 m/s in field and wind-tunnel trials - adequate for many meteorological uses at a fraction of sonic-anemometer cost.
Inference: Research-grade wind measurement can be cheap and robust (no moving parts).
Supports_This_Project: Candidate low-cost wind sensor for the planned wind-speed channel at deployment sites, consistent with our low-cost philosophy.

Name: A Statistical Study of Wind Speed and its Connectivity with Relative Humidity and Temperature in Ughelli, Nigeria
Abstract: Siloko & Uddin (2023) apply Gaussian kernel-density analysis to 5 years of wind speed data and its correlation with humidity and temperature at one Nigerian site.
What_They_Do: Siloko & Uddin (2023) apply Gaussian kernel-density analysis to 5 years of wind speed data and its correlation with humidity and temperature at one Nigerian site.
Results: Wind-temperature correlation consistently negative; wind-humidity correlation flips sign between years.
Inference: Wind statistics and their couplings to other weather variables are site- and season-specific and unstable across years.
Supports_This_Project: Background caution: wind-related features/corrections learned in one campaign or climate may not transfer - supports our campaign-aware validation splits.

Name: The Performance Measurement Test on Rain Gauge of Tipping Bucket due to Controlling of the Water Flow Rate
Abstract: Prakosa et al. (ElConRus 2018) lab-test tipping-bucket repeatability by feeding controlled flow rates (24-100 ml/min) via a PWM-controlled pump and counting tips.
File: research papers\Paper_RainGaugeTippingBucketIEEE.pdf (paper at PDF pages 30-34 of an ElConRus 2018 proceedings extract)
What_They_Do: Prakosa et al. (ElConRus 2018) lab-test tipping-bucket repeatability by feeding controlled flow rates (24-100 ml/min) via a PWM-controlled pump and counting tips.
Results: Volume per tip varied across series: 18.93+/-0.60, 17.89+/-0.92, 17.80+/-0.60 ml/tip (avg 18.21+/-1.25, ~7% spread) - flow rate significantly affects calibration.
Inference: The mm-per-tip constant of a tipping bucket is intensity-dependent; ground-truth conversion drifts a few percent with rain rate.
Supports_This_Project: Another quantified label-error source, biased exactly at high intensities where our model struggles; supports dynamic calibration corrections on gauge labels.

Name: AI Methods in Sensor Calibration (Review)
Abstract: Kou et al. (Sensors 2026) review AI replacing polynomial transfer-function fitting in sensor calibration: learned input-output mappings, compensation of temperature/humidity/drift, enabling low-cost sensor fleets, plus preprocessing/augmentation tooling.
What_They_Do: Kou et al. (Sensors 2026) review AI replacing polynomial transfer-function fitting in sensor calibration: learned input-output mappings, compensation of temperature/humidity/drift, enabling low-cost sensor fleets, plus preprocessing/augmentation tooling.
Results: Across many sensor types AI calibration beats preset empirical models on accuracy and stability, especially for nonlinear transfer functions and environmental compensation; generalization and long-term drift remain open.
Inference: This project is an instance of AI sensor calibration: the microphone is the raw sensor, the ML model its learned transfer function to mm/h, and cross-campaign compensation is the hard part AI handles best.
Supports_This_Project: Framing for the paper's related work and a menu of interference-compensation and augmentation techniques for cross-campaign robustness.

Name: Guide to Meteorological Instruments and Methods of Observation (WMO-No. 8, 7th ed., 2008)
Abstract: The WMO standard reference defining how precipitation and all meteorological variables must be measured: siting, exposure, gauge types, uncertainty requirements, QC procedures, wind-undercatch corrections.
What_They_Do: The WMO standard reference defining how precipitation and all meteorological variables must be measured: siting, exposure, gauge types, uncertainty requirements, QC procedures, wind-undercatch corrections.
Results: Defines the accuracy classes and siting/exposure rules official rain measurements follow worldwide.
Inference: Claims that an acoustic gauge matches official measurement quality must be phrased against WMO-No. 8 requirements.
Supports_This_Project: Compliance yardstick: target uncertainty specification, ground-truth siting justification, and standard undercatch corrections for our tipping-bucket labels.

Name: RHD Rain Disdrometer and Hail Detection Sensor (commercial datasheet - NOT a research paper)
Abstract: Commercial low-cost acoustic rain gauge/disdrometer: sealed 160 mm stainless hemisphere sensing drop/hail impacts; no moving parts, <1 mA standby; analog/SDI-12/RS-232/Modbus outputs.
File: research papers\Rain disdrometer and hail detection.pdf = research\Category 1\Datasheet_RHD_V1_41.pdf (byte-identical)
What_They_Do: Commercial low-cost acoustic rain gauge/disdrometer: sealed 160 mm stainless hemisphere sensing drop/hail impacts; no moving parts, <1 mA standby; analog/SDI-12/RS-232/Modbus outputs.
Results: Spec: rain intensity accuracy +/-15%; DSD in 27 classes (<=0.75 to >=7.0 mm, ~0.5 mm threshold); hail counting to 5 impacts/s; drop velocity not measured.
Inference: Industry already sells impact-acoustic rain sensing at +/-15% accuracy but requires a dedicated engineered surface - that is the commercial bar for a microphone-only ML approach.
Supports_This_Project: Competitive benchmark (+/-15%), proof of commercial demand, and a target feature set (27-class DSD output) for the long-term roadmap.

Name: A Multi-Sensor Comparative Analysis on the Suitability of Generated DEM from Sentinel-1 SAR Interferometry (OFF-TOPIC)
Abstract: Mohammadi et al. (2020) generate a DEM from Sentinel-1 InSAR for sites in Malaysia and Iran and compare against AIRSAR, ALOS-PALSAR, TanDEM-X and SRTM using statistical and hydrological criteria.
What_They_Do: Mohammadi et al. (2020) generate a DEM from Sentinel-1 InSAR for sites in Malaysia and Iran and compare against AIRSAR, ALOS-PALSAR, TanDEM-X and SRTM using statistical and hydrological criteria.
Results: Sentinel-1 DEM is noisy (short perpendicular baseline) and performs worse than SRTM and TanDEM-X despite finer nominal resolution.
Inference: Off-topic for acoustic rainfall sensing; likely collected as general hydrology background.
Supports_This_Project: No direct support - recommend moving out of the rainfall library so it does not pollute searches.

Name: Utilizing Piezo Acoustic Sensors for the Identification of Surface Roughness and Textures (tangential)
Abstract: Kursun et al. (2022) correlate frequency spectra from piezo disks (elastic waves from a stylus dragged on metal) with profilometer surface-roughness parameters Ra/Rz.
What_They_Do: Kursun et al. (2022) correlate frequency spectra from piezo disks (elastic waves from a stylus dragged on metal) with profilometer surface-roughness parameters Ra/Rz.
Results: Clear correlations between piezo-acoustic spectra and traditional roughness measurements; promising novel roughness measurement method.
Inference: Manufacturing metrology, not meteorology - but independently demonstrates that impact/friction acoustics plus spectral analysis yields quantitative physical parameters.
Supports_This_Project: Methodological inspiration only, for piezo-based sensing-surface variants of the acoustic gauge.

Name: A self-powered microsystem with efficient power management for continuous wireless sensing (deployment-adjacent)
Abstract: Zhao et al. (2026) power a continuously sensing wireless microsystem entirely from a triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) harvesting <10 Hz mechanical energy, with power management fixing the TENG-electronics impedance mismatch.
What_They_Do: Zhao et al. (2026) power a continuously sensing wireless microsystem entirely from a triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) harvesting <10 Hz mechanical energy, with power management fixing the TENG-electronics impedance mismatch.
Results: 5x energy gain over full-bridge rectification; cold-start 0 to 4.2 V in 525 s; ~110 uW delivered at 5 Hz excitation; battery-less wireless gas monitor demonstrated.
Inference: Battery-less power budgets (~110 uW) are far below continuous audio-ML capture needs today, but energy-autonomous nodes (incl. raindrop TENGs) are maturing.
Supports_This_Project: Deployment engineering reference if the project explores energy-autonomous field nodes; not a rainfall-estimation reference.

Name: Numerical simulation studies of rain gage data correction due to wind effect
Abstract: Habib, Krajewski, Nespor & Kruger (1999) use CFD-derived wind-flow-around-gage correction formulae with high-resolution rainfall/wind data, focused on how the temporal averaging scale affects the estimated wind-induced bias.
File: research papers\1999 - Numerical simulation studies of rain gage data correction due to wind effect.pdf
What_They_Do: Habib, Krajewski, Nespor & Kruger (1999) use CFD-derived wind-flow-around-gage correction formulae with high-resolution rainfall/wind data, focused on how the temporal averaging scale affects the estimated wind-induced bias.
Results: Correcting at short timescales (down to 1 min) is essential - averaging first significantly overestimates the wind bias. The error is a nonlinear function of wind speed, rain rate, AND drop-size distribution; wind-speed-only correction factors show large scatter vs literature.
Inference: A wind correction applied at coarse time resolution is actively harmful, not just imprecise; wind speed alone is an incomplete correction variable without DSD information.
Supports_This_Project: Informs how to add a wind channel: correction/features must operate at our native short-clip resolution, and a simple wind-speed feature alone may underperform without a DSD proxy (which the acoustic spectrum itself may supply).

Name: Bias correction of global daily rain gauge measurements
Abstract: Ungersbock, Rubel, Fuchs & Rudolf (2001) develop a statistical model (for BALTEX/GPCC) correcting DAILY rain-gauge bias instead of only monthly correction factors, validated across 4 GEWEX continental regions.
File: research papers\2001 - Bias correction of global daily rain gauge measurements.pdf
What_They_Do: Ungersbock, Rubel, Fuchs & Rudolf (2001) develop a statistical model (for BALTEX/GPCC) correcting DAILY rain-gauge bias instead of only monthly correction factors, validated across 4 GEWEX continental regions.
Results: Daily corrections roughly match monthly Legates (1987) corrections in Europe in summer but diverge elsewhere: Legates over-corrects snow by up to 50% and over-corrects Asia/South-America by 50-100%, while matching closely in North America.
Inference: Gauge correction factors are not universal - they vary by region, season, and precipitation phase; coarse monthly corrections can be badly wrong for individual days.
Supports_This_Project: Reinforces that any correction to tipping-bucket ground truth must be fine-grained (temporal + regional), consistent with our existing campaign-aware treatment of 19 field deployments.

Name: Corrections of rainfall data obtained by tipping bucket rain gauge
Abstract: Maksimovic, Busek & Petrovic (1991) lab/field-test a Rimco tipping-bucket gauge to characterize non-linearity (tip volume depends on intensity), 'double tipping' after successive tips, and calibration-volume sensitivity.
File: research papers\1991 - Corrections of rainfall data obtained by tipping bucket rain gauge.pdf
What_They_Do: Maksimovic, Busek & Petrovic (1991) lab/field-test a Rimco tipping-bucket gauge to characterize non-linearity (tip volume depends on intensity), 'double tipping' after successive tips, and calibration-volume sensitivity.
Results: Presents a full experimental correction methodology so raw field tips can be corrected for these non-linear, intensity-dependent effects.
Inference: The mm-per-tip conversion is not constant even within one gauge - it depends on current rain intensity, and errors compound at high intensity.
Supports_This_Project: Second independent confirmation (with Prakosa et al. 2018) that tipping-bucket ground truth carries intensity-dependent systematic error, strengthening the case for treating gauge labels as noisy, especially at high rain rates where our model already underperforms.

Name: Rainfall and sampling uncertainties: A rain gauge perspective
Abstract: Villarini, Mandapaka, Krajewski & Moore (2008) use 6+ years from a dense 50-gauge network (135 km2, Brue catchment, England) to characterize temporal sampling uncertainty (observation gaps) and spatial sampling uncertainty (point vs areal estimate).
File: research papers\2008 - Rainfall and sampling uncertainties A rain gauge perspective.pdf
What_They_Do: Villarini, Mandapaka, Krajewski & Moore (2008) use 6+ years from a dense 50-gauge network (135 km2, Brue catchment, England) to characterize temporal sampling uncertainty (observation gaps) and spatial sampling uncertainty (point vs areal estimate).
Results: Temporal sampling uncertainty grows with sampling interval per a scaling law and shrinks with larger averaging area; spatial sampling uncertainty shrinks with longer accumulation time. Derives a rule for how many gauges are needed for a given areal-accuracy target.
Inference: Comparing a point sensor (gauge or single microphone) to an areal ground truth carries an intrinsic, quantifiable representativeness error separate from instrument error.
Supports_This_Project: Gives vocabulary/framework for a limitation we face implicitly: a single microphone, like a single gauge, is a point measurement, relevant when discussing area-coverage value propositions or non-co-located gauge comparisons.

Name: Rainfall Variability and its Effect on Rainfall Measurements
Abstract: Steiner, Smith, Uijlenhoet & Hou (AMS preprint P14.1) analyze 30 storms over the densely-instrumented Goodwin Creek watershed (Mississippi) to separate how much radar-vs-gauge disagreement is due to sensor-resolution differences versus genuine small-scale rainfall variability.
File: research papers\n.d. - Rainfall variability and its effect on rainfall measurements.pdf
What_They_Do: Steiner, Smith, Uijlenhoet & Hou (AMS preprint P14.1) analyze 30 storms over the densely-instrumented Goodwin Creek watershed (Mississippi) to separate how much radar-vs-gauge disagreement is due to sensor-resolution differences versus genuine small-scale rainfall variability.
Results: High-quality gauge data is essential for radar bias-adjustment - poor gauge data can dramatically distort merged rainfall products; a meaningful share of radar-gauge disagreement is attributable to real spatiotemporal rainfall variability, not instrument error.
Inference: Disagreement between two well-functioning rainfall instruments does not automatically mean one is broken - some of it is genuine physical small-scale variability of rain.
Supports_This_Project: Caution for evaluating our acoustic-gauge-vs-tipping-bucket comparison: some residual error should be attributed to genuine micro-scale rainfall variability between what the mic hears locally and what a (possibly non-co-located) gauge collects, not solely to model inaccuracy.

Name: The quantification and correction of wind-induced precipitation measurement errors
Abstract: Kochendorfer, Rasmussen, Wolff et al. (2017, NOAA/NCAR/Norwegian Met Institute) use two WMO-SPICE-affiliated testbeds to quantify wind-induced undercatch for unshielded and shielded weighing gauges, deriving correction functions using only wind speed and air temperature.
File: research papers\2017 - The quantification and correction of wind-induced precipitation measurement errors.pdf
What_They_Do: Kochendorfer, Rasmussen, Wolff et al. (2017, NOAA/NCAR/Norwegian Met Institute) use two WMO-SPICE-affiliated testbeds to quantify wind-induced undercatch for unshielded and shielded weighing gauges, deriving correction functions using only wind speed and air temperature.
Results: Unshielded gauges catch under 50% of actual solid precipitation above 5 m/s wind. A single correction function for single-Alter-shielded gauges, tested at two independent sites, reduced bias from -12% to 0% (US) and -27% to -4% (Norway).
Inference: Wind-induced undercatch is large but well-characterized and correctable with two cheap ubiquitous inputs (wind speed, temperature), and the correction transfers across very different climates.
Supports_This_Project: Strongest quantitative evidence in the library for prioritizing wind speed as our next lever: shows a large, well-characterized, transferable bias with a simple two-input correction function as precedent for a wind-augmented version of our model.

Name: Variability of Drop Size Distributions: Time-Scale Dependence of the Variability and Its Effects on Rain Estimation
Abstract: Lee & Zawadzki (2005, McGill) analyze 5 years (20,000+ one-minute DSDs) of Montreal disdrometer data to quantify how DSD variability at multiple time scales biases radar R-Z rain-rate estimation, classifying DSDs by underlying physical process.
File: research papers\2005 - Variability of Drop Size Distributions Time-Scale Dependence of the Variability and Its Effects on Rain Estimation.pdf
What_They_Do: Lee & Zawadzki (2005, McGill) analyze 5 years (20,000+ one-minute DSDs) of Montreal disdrometer data to quantify how DSD variability at multiple time scales biases radar R-Z rain-rate estimation, classifying DSDs by underlying physical process.
Results: A single climatological R-Z relationship gives ~41% random error in instantaneous rain rate; daily accumulations carry a 28% bias from day-to-day DSD variability; variability BETWEEN physical processes (e.g. stratiform vs convective) causes a 41% accumulation bias - accurate estimation (~7% error) needs identifying the correct physical process first.
Inference: A single static mapping (R-Z for radar, or by analogy audio-features-to-intensity for us) has an irreducible ~30-40% error ceiling unless conditioned on rain-event type/DSD regime, not just current intensity.
Supports_This_Project: The most important physics paper in this batch for model design: strong evidence that a single audio-to-intensity regression function is fundamentally limited by unmodeled DSD/rain-type variability, and that conditioning the model on rain regime (mixture-of-experts / regime-conditioned regression) is a concrete, literature-backed idea worth prototyping against our stagnant 0.531 R2.
